behaviors.description: Only files changed in the pull request are analyzed; unchanged files are not scanned.
behaviors.justification: Real failure: code reads all files instead of using the changed-files filter. A unit test mocking 'changed files' won't catch file-discovery bugs where the code bypasses the filter. Integration testing requires a test filesystem (multiple files), the actual file discovery + filtering logic, and mocked GitHub API to verify unchanged files are skipped.
behaviors.test_description: Create a test directory with 10 files, mock GitHub API to return 5 as changed, run the actual file discovery and filtering logic, verify that exactly 5 files are analyzed and 5 are skipped.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test requires a real test filesystem and actual filtering logic wired together—Integration is appropriate.

behaviors.description: Findings are grouped by severity (error, warning, info) in the posted PR comment.
behaviors.justification: The behavior spans grouping logic plus the GitHub posting integration. A unit test of just the grouping function would miss failures like malformed JSON in the comment or failed API calls. Integration testing verifies the end-to-end pipeline: findings → grouped formatting → posted to mocked GitHub.
behaviors.test_description: Create findings with 3 errors, 2 warnings, 1 info. Run grouping and formatting logic, mock GitHub API, verify the posted comment shows findings in severity order without corruption.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test requires wiring grouping logic with mocked GitHub posting—Integration is appropriate.

behaviors.description: A pull request touching 200 files completes analysis within the 10-minute Actions timeout.
behaviors.justification: Real failure is performance degradation (O(n²) loops, slow I/O, subprocess overhead). Unit tests with mocked/instant operations cannot reveal actual performance. System testing with real file I/O (test directory with 200 files) and real subprocess execution of the linter captures actual elapsed time.
behaviors.test_description: Create a test directory with 200 realistic files (various types, nested structure). Run the actual linter subprocess on all files, measure wall-clock time, verify completion within 10 minutes. Include edge cases like deeply nested directories.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test requires real file I/O and actual subprocess execution to measure real performance—System is necessary.

behaviors.description: When the linter process crashes on a malformed file, remaining files are still analyzed and results are posted.
behaviors.justification: Real failure involves subprocess crash handling at OS level: exit codes, signal handling, resource cleanup, partial state recovery. Mocking a function-level crash would miss subprocess-specific behaviors. System testing with actual subprocess execution and a genuinely malformed file (or realistic crash simulation) is required.
behaviors.test_description: Create 10 test files with one intentionally malformed file that crashes the linter subprocess. Run the pipeline and verify: (a) crash is caught and process continues, (b) remaining 9 files are analyzed, (c) results including the crash error are posted to mocked GitHub.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test requires real subprocess execution and OS-level error handling—System is appropriate.

behaviors.description: Duplicate findings on the same line from overlapping rules are deduplicated in the output.
behaviors.justification: This is pure deduplication logic—a function taking a findings list and returning unique entries. No I/O, no subprocess, no integration needed. A unit test with sample findings (duplicates and unique entries) verifies the logic in isolation.
behaviors.test_description: Create a findings list with duplicates (same file, same line, same message from overlapping rules) and unique entries. Run deduplication logic and verify output contains exactly one entry per unique file:line:message combination.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test requires only the deduplication function, no external dependencies—Unit is appropriate.
